core_claim: UnDefend is a denial-of-service and local privilege escalation tool that blocks Microsoft Defender definition updates or silently disables the antivirus. Released by Nightmare Eclipse as part of the June 2026 vulnerability wave.

slides.on_slide_copy: UNDEFEND SILENCES DEFENDER. NO UPDATE. NO ALERT.
slides.subline: DoS + LPE tool. Blocks definition updates. Disables AV silently. Nightmare Eclipse exploit three.

caption:
Nightmare Eclipse released UnDefend as the third exploit in their June 2026 wave. A dual-mode denial-of-service and LPE tool that blocks Microsoft Defender definition updates or silently disables the antivirus entirely.

Two modes: (1) Update blockade — targets the Windows Update service (wuauserv) and Defender's cloud connectivity. Definitions freeze. Cloud lookups fail. The AV goes blind. (2) Silent disable — manipulates the tamper protection registry key via a privileged file write. Stops the Antimalware Service Executable (MsMpEng.exe). No toast notification. No event log entry.

The mechanism exploits a Windows Filtering Platform (WFP) callout driver flaw. Intercepts Defender's update traffic at kernel level. Drops packets silently. Second path bypasses tamper protection via registry manipulation. No reboot needed.

Defender shows green. 'No threats found.' 'Protection up to date.' But definitions are months old. Cloud protection is offline. Tamper protection is bypassed. No alert fires. Event logs show service stops as 'expected.' Defenders trust the green checkmark. The checkmark lies.

No vendor patch. Tamper protection can be bypassed. Defenders must verify Defender is actually working: monitor service health independently, alert on definition age exceeding 24 hours, validate cloud connectivity, hunt for WFP callout driver loads.

A green checkmark is not proof of protection. It is a UI state that can be decoupled from the engine.

How do you verify Defender is actually updating and running?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.

[UnDefend, Nightmare Eclipse, Windows zero-day, Microsoft Defender, denial of service, tamper protection bypass, WFP callout driver, definition updates, silent disable, endpoint validation]
comment_prompt: How do you verify Defender is actually updating and running?
alt_text: Slide 1 (cover): a Defender shield icon in the dark, its update pulse flatlining into a straight red line, the shield fading from blue to grey.
Slide 2 (context): a dark Windows Security center, 'Virus & threat protection' showing 'No action needed' but a red 'Definitions: 0 days old' warning.
Slide 3 (risk): two service icons in the dark — wuauserv and MsMpEng — both with red stop symbols pulsing.
Slide 4 (mechanism): a network packet in the dark, a WFP callout driver catching it and dissolving it into red dust before it reaches Defender.
Slide 5 (gap): a Windows Security dashboard, all green checkmarks, but a faint red data stream leaking from the bottom edge unseen.
Slide 6 (defense): split composition, left side Defender with live pulse and cloud connection, right side flatlined with red kernel driver loaded beneath.
Slide 7 (takeaway): a Defender shield icon, half glowing blue with live pulse, half faded grey with red kernel driver beneath.
Slide 8 (cta): a custom monitoring dashboard showing Defender definition age and service status on a clean dark end-card.

sources.source: Nightmare Eclipse blog: UnDefend Technical Details
sources.supports: Dual-mode operation (update blockade + silent disable); WFP callout driver flaw; tamper protection registry manipulation; MsMpEng and wuauserv targeting; works on fully patched Win 10/11; no CVE assigned; no alert generation; event log evasion.

sources.source: SecurityWeek: Nightmare Eclipse Releases Six Windows Zero-Days

narration.text: UnDefend silences Defender. No update. No alert. DoS plus LPE. Nightmare Eclipse exploit three.
Dual mode: blocks definition updates indefinitely or disables AV silently. Works on patched Windows 10 and 11.
Targets wuauserv and MsMpEng. Update blockade freezes definitions. Silent disable stops the service. No toast. No event log.
WFP callout driver drops update packets at kernel level. Registry manipulation bypasses tamper protection. No reboot.
Defender shows green. Definitions months old. Cloud offline. Tamper protection bypassed. Checkmark lies.
No patch. Monitor service health. Alert on definition age. Validate cloud. Hunt WFP drivers.
A green checkmark is not proof of protection. It is a UI state decoupled from the engine.
How do you verify Defender is actually running? Tell me in the comments.

beats.caption: UnDefend silences Defender. DoS plus LPE.
Dual mode. Update blockade. Silent disable. Patched hit.
wuauserv and MsMpEng targeted. No toast. No event log.
WFP driver drops packets. Registry bypasses tamper protection.
Green checkmark. Definitions old. Cloud dead. Checkmark lies.
Monitor health. Alert age. Validate cloud. Hunt drivers.
Checkmark not protection. UI state decoupled.
